If none, write `None`. - Linux now has a native GTK4 + Libadwaita companion app in-repo. - Ubuntu GNOME users get tray/status integration through the companion plus the private AppIndicator helper. - The Linux app can discover and work with the user `openclaw-gateway.service` lifecycle through native systemd integration. - The app surfaces first-run progression clearly across the main readiness states: - before `openclaw setup` - after `openclaw setup` but before gateway install - after gateway install with the service active and the gateway answering - The app surfaces runtime/readiness/onboarding/diagnostics states directly in the Linux UI. - Channels can now be inspected and managed in-app, including probe, logout, config editing, and WhatsApp QR linking flow. - Skills can now be filtered and managed in-app, including enable/disable, install, and env/API-key updates. - Sessions can now be managed in-app, including patch/reset/compact/delete and log access. - Cron can now be managed in-app, including scheduler status, recent runs, create/edit, run-now, enable/disable, delete, and transcript access. - Instances now expose richer remote node details, pending pairing approval/reject flows, and copy-debug-info behavior. - Log/transcript opening is locality-aware: local resources open locally when available, otherwise the UI falls back safely. - Channel config editing now validates edited JSON, preserves unrelated config keys, and saves using `baseHash`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: [user on Linux] -> [CLI / dashboard / partial prototype paths] -> [limited lifecycle visibility and little or no native management] After: [user on Linux] -> [native GTK4 companion app] -> [systemd + HTTP + authenticated WebSocket runtime model] -> [tray / diagnostics / onboarding / management sections] -> [native actions for channels, skills, sessions, cron, instances] ```` ## Security Impact (required) * New permissions/capabilities? target.title: Add user chat bubble color selector for macOS application
target.body: # Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: The macOS chat bubble color is hardcoded to a static blue, with no way for users to personalize it. - Why it matters: Users want visual customization of the chat interface to match their preferences or distinguish between instances. - What changed: Added a new "Colors" settings tab with a 10-color palette picker, a custom `ColorPicker`, and a system accent color toggle. `ColorPreferencesStore` (in `OpenClawKit`) persists preferences via `UserDefaults` and exposes a `resolvedColor` that `ChatTheme.userBubble` now reads on macOS. `userText` dynamically picks black or white based on WCAG relative luminance. A cross-platform `ColorHexSupport` utility was added to `OpenClawKit` for hex-to-Color and Color-to-hex conversions. The macOS-local `ColorHexSupport` gained a reverse `hex(from:)` helper. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): iOS chat bubble color remains the existing static blue. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) N/A ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - macOS: New "Colors" tab in Settings (between "Channels" and "Skills") with three selection modes: system accent color (default, preserves existing behavior), palette color (10 curated colors from Apple's Human Interface Guidelines), or arbitrary custom color via `ColorPicker`. - Chat bubble color updates live when a new color is selected; preferences persist across app restarts via `UserDefaults`. - User message text automatically switches between white and black depending on the chosen bubble color's luminance (WCAG threshold at 0.6). ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: [user message] -> [hardcoded rgb(127,184,212) bubble] -> [white text] After: [user message] -> [ColorPreferencesStore.resolvedColor] -> [palette / custom / accent bubble] -> [WCAG luminance > 0.6?] -> yes -> [black text] -> no -> [white text] ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS 15+ - Runtime/container: Swift 6 / SwiftUI - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): N/A ### Steps 1. Build and launch the macOS app. 2. Open Settings > Colors. 3. Select a palette color, apply a custom color, or toggle "Use Accent Color." 4. Open a chat session see the selected color on your messages. ### Expected - User chat bubble reflects the selected color. - Text is white on dark bubbles and black on light bubbles (e.g. yellow). - Preference survives app restart. ### Actual - Verified as expected. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) **Color palette** **Custom color** **Dark text on bright color** ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: Palette selection, custom color via ColorPicker, accent color toggle, preference persistence across restart, live bubble color update in chat. - Edge cases checked: Light colors (yellow, orange) produce black text; dark colors (indigo, blue) produce white text. Clearing custom color falls back to palette or accent. Off-main-thread `resolvedColor` defaults to light palette variant. - What you did **not** verify: iOS runtime (feature is macOS-only for now). No automated Swift tests were added for the new views or store. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: `ColorPreferencesStore.shared` is `nonisolated(unsafe)` and initialized eagerly in `AppState.init`; concurrent first access from a background thread could race. - Mitigation: `AppState` calls `_ = ColorPreferencesStore.shared` during init on the main actor, ensuring the singleton is created before any background reader. - Risk: `UserDefaults`-backed preferences do not sync across macOS reinstalls or profiles. - Mitigation: Defaults to system accent color (existing behavior); preferences are non-critical and easily re-set.

target.title: feat(plugins): add optional api.resetSession()
target.body:
## Summary

`main` already has gateway-side session reset support through `performGatewaySessionReset()`.

This PR adds optional plugin-side `api.resetSession(key, reason?)` support on top of the current mainline architecture.

- Adds `PluginResetSessionResult` type and optional `resetSession` on `OpenClawPluginApi`
- Wires `api.resetSession(...)` in the plugin registry only in full registration mode and only when the gateway exposes `sessions.reset`
- Reuses gateway-owned canonicalization and reset resolution (no plugin-side duplicate logic)
- Unifies config snapshot usage in `performGatewaySessionReset` to eliminate config-drift between target resolution and entry lookup
- Adds per-canonical-key in-flight guard in the reset service
- Exports `PluginResetSessionResult` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core`

## Scope

This PR:
- adds `PluginResetSessionResult` to `src/plugins/types.ts`
- adds optional `resetSession` to `OpenClawPluginApi`
- adds builder support in `src/plugins/api-builder.ts`
- wires `api.resetSession(...)` in `src/plugins/registry.ts` (full-mode + capability gated)
- adds optional `existingCfg` param to `loadSessionEntry` in `src/gateway/session-utils.ts`
- adds in-flight guard and unified resolution in `src/gateway/session-reset-service.ts`
- exports the result type from `src/plugin-sdk/core.ts`
- adds focused plugin contract tests (17 tests)
- refreshes generated plugin SDK API baselines

This PR does not:
- change gateway reset RPC behavior
- broaden session architecture scope beyond the plugin API surface
- revive any stale-branch refactor baggage from the closed #43375

## Behavior

`api.resetSession(key, reason?)`:
- accepts `"new"` or `"reset"` reason values (defaults to `"new"`)
- trims and validates the key
- delegates to gateway-owned `performGatewaySessionReset`
- resolves to a structured plugin result instead of rejecting for operational failures
- absent in setup-only, setup-runtime, and cli-metadata registration modes
- absent when gateway does not expose `sessions.reset`

Success: `{ ok: true, key, sessionId }`
Failure: `{ ok: false, key, code, message }`

## Local Verification

```
pnpm test -- src/plugins/reset-session.test.ts     # 17/17 pass
pnpm test -- src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts      # 77/77 pass
pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check                           # OK
pnpm tsgo                                           # no new errors
```

Replaces #43375 (closed, stale branch).

target.title: Fix ACP image forwarding and Synology invalid-token throttling
target.body:
## Summary

This PR bundles two small, low-risk fixes:

1. Fix ACP attachment collection so URL-only image attachments are forwarded into ACP turns.
2. Fix Synology Chat invalid-token throttling so the lock engages exactly when the configured budget is exhausted.

## What changed

### ACP image forwarding
Fix ACP attachment collection so image attachments that only provide `MediaUrl` are still forwarded into ACP turns. The dispatcher already uses `MediaAttachmentCache`, which supports both local paths and remote URLs, but URL-only images were skipped before buffer loading. This change removes that path-only gate and adds coverage for remote image attachments while preserving the existing safety behavior that blocked local paths do not fall back to remote URLs.

### Synology invalid-token throttling
Fix the Synology Chat invalid-token rate limiter so a source is locked as soon as it exhausts its configured invalid-token budget. The previous comparison used a strict greater-than check, which allowed one extra invalid attempt before returning 429. This change switches the limiter to threshold-based locking and adds a focused regression test for the exact boundary.

## Validation

- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts extensions/synology-chat/src/webhook-handler.test.ts`

Result on 2026-05-12 after rebasing onto `origin/main` `5abb4b18c8`:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)  # auto-reply shard
Tests       41 passed (41)

Test Files  1 passed (1)  # extension-messaging shard
Tests       23 passed (23)
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** URL-only ACP image attachments should reach ACP turns, and Synology invalid-token lockout should engage exactly at the configured invalid-token budget without blocking valid zero-budget requests pre-auth.

**Real environment tested:** Local Linux checkout of OpenClaw on branch head `ff7aee2b89`, Node/tsx runtime from the repository dependency set, after rebasing onto `origin/main` `5abb4b18c8`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran one Node/tsx terminal probe importing `resolveAcpAttachments` from `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-attachments.ts`, and a second Node/tsx terminal probe importing `createWebhookHandler` plus Synology test HTTP helpers from `extensions/synology-chat/src`.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the ACP attachment probe:

```json
{
  "cacheCalls": [
    {
      "url": "https://example.com/inbound.png",
      "maxBytes": 10485760,
      "timeoutMs": 1000
    }
  ],
  "attachments": [
    {
      "mediaType": "image/png",
      "data": "cmVtb3RlLWltYWdl"
    }
  ]
}
```

Terminal output from the Synology webhook probe:

```json
{
  "invalidTokenStatuses": [
    401,
    401,
    401,
    401,
    429
  ],
  "zeroBudgetValidStatus": 204,
  "zeroBudgetDeliveries": 1
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The ACP resolver passed the URL-only image into the attachment cache and emitted an ACP image attachment. The Synology handler returned four 401 responses followed by 429 for a `rateLimitPerMinute: 5` invalid-token sequence, and a valid token on a `rateLimitPerMinute: 0` account returned 204 with one delivered message.

**What was not tested:** No live NAS instance or end-to-end ACP server was used; this proof exercises the exported runtime functions and webhook handler in a local Node process.

target.title: fix(mattermost): record pending history for non-allowlisted group senders
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** When `groupPolicy: "allowlist"`, messages from non-allowlisted senders are dropped at the access-check stage before `recordPendingHistoryEntryIfEnabled()` is called. The bot loses all conversation context from those users.
- **Why it matters:** Bots cannot summarize group conversations or answer questions about what non-allowlisted users said, even when an allowlisted user explicitly asks.
- **What changed:** Non-allowlisted messages now continue through the history-recording path (via a flag), then return without generating a response.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Access control behavior is unchanged — non-allowlisted users still cannot trigger bot responses. Only pending history recording is affected.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #57607
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** The `GROUP_POLICY_NOT_ALLOWLISTED` branch in the access-check block returns immediately, bypassing `recordPendingHistory()`. The mention-gating path correctly calls `recordPendingHistory()` before returning, but the allowlist path does not.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No test verifies that pending history is recorded for dropped-but-visible group messages.
- **Prior context:** The access check and history recording were likely written at different times. The mention-gating path was added later with the correct pattern.
- **Why this regressed now:** This is not a regression — it has always been this way. The behavior becomes noticeable when users rely on `groupPolicy: "allowlist"` for access control while expecting the bot to maintain full group context.
- **If unknown, what was ruled out:** N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** `monitor.channel-kind.test.ts` or `monitor.authz.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** When a non-allowlisted sender posts in a group with `groupPolicy: "allowlist"`, the message should be recorded to `channelHistories` but no response should be generated.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** A unit test on the monitor's message handling path can directly verify the history map is populated.
- **Existing test that already covers this:** None found.
- **If no new test is added, why not:** The existing test infrastructure for the monitor is complex. Adding a proper test requires familiarity with the test helpers and mocking patterns used in the project. Happy to add one with maintainer guidance.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Bots using `groupPolicy: "allowlist"` can now see messages from all group participants in their conversation context.
- Non-allowlisted users still cannot trigger bot responses (no change).
- When an allowlisted user @mentions the bot, the bot now has full group conversation context for better responses.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[non-allowlisted sender posts] -> [access check: NOT_ALLOWLISTED] -> [return] (no history recorded)
[allowlisted user asks "summarize"] -> [bot has no context] -> [cannot summarize]

After:
[non-allowlisted sender posts] -> [access check: set flag] -> [recordPendingHistory()] -> [return] (no response)
[allowlisted user asks "summarize"] -> [bot has full context] -> [accurate summary]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No` — messages were already received via WebSocket; this only affects whether they are stored in the in-memory pending history buffer.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.4 (Apple Silicon)
- Runtime/container: Node.js 25.8.1
- Model/provider: openai/gpt-5.4
- Integration/channel: Mattermost
- Relevant config (redacted):
  ```json
  {
    "channels": {
      "mattermost": {
        "chatmode": "oncall",
        "groupPolicy": "allowlist",
        "groupAllowFrom": ["<user-id>"],
        "historyLimit": 50
      }
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure Mattermost with `groupPolicy: "allowlist"` and one user in `groupAllowFrom`.
2. Have a non-allowlisted user send messages in the group.
3. Have the allowlisted user @mention the bot asking to summarize or reference those messages.

### Expected

Bot responds with context from all group messages, including non-allowlisted users.

### Actual

Before fix: Bot has no context of non-allowlisted user messages.
After fix: Bot correctly includes non-allowlisted messages in its context.

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets

Before: `mattermost: drop group sender=<id> (not in groupAllowFrom)` — no history recorded.
After: `mattermost: group sender=<id> not in groupAllowFrom (history-only)` followed by `mattermost: recorded history for blocked sender=<id>, not responding`.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** Tested with two Mattermost bot accounts, one allowlisted user, and multiple non-allowlisted users in a shared group channel. Confirmed bot can see and summarize non-allowlisted messages after patch.
- **Edge cases checked:** Verified non-allowlisted users still cannot trigger responses via @mention. Verified DM policy is unaffected. Verified other group policy modes (disabled, open) are unaffected.
- **What you did not verify:** Other channel plugins (Telegram, Signal, etc.) — this change only touches the Mattermost monitor.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** Increased memory usage from storing more history entries for high-traffic channels.
  - **Mitigation:** Bounded by the existing `historyLimit` setting (default 50). No unbounded growth.
- **Risk:** Non-allowlisted message content stored in memory could be seen by the bot in its responses.
  - **Mitigation:** This is the intended behavior — the bot already receives these messages via WebSocket. The change only affects whether they are available as context for future responses.

target.title: fix(gateway): preserve indentation when recombining non-streaming replies
target.body:
AI-assisted: yes (OpenClaw agent-authored).
Testing: targeted local validation (gateway regression tests + targeted lint + pre-commit repo checks).

## Summary

- Companion to merged #55960, not a duplicate: #55960 fixed indentation loss while stripping inline reply directives in `src/utils/directive-tags.ts`; this PR fixes the remaining gateway-layer loss when non-streaming reply parts are recombined in `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`.
- Preserve each non-streaming payload chunk as authored when recombining it, so indentation-sensitive content survives payload boundaries.
- Reuse that same combiner for `/btw` side results so sibling non-streaming gateway paths behave consistently.
- Normalize `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` boundary trimming and add focused regression coverage for the single-newline, `/btw`, and CRLF cases.
- Scope boundary: no streaming-path formatting changes, no Markdown parsing changes, and no channel-specific formatting rules.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #42576
- Related #29377
- Companion to merged #55960

## Relationship to #55960

Merged PR #55960 fixed one real whitespace-loss layer: inline reply-directive stripping in the shared parser (`src/utils/directive-tags.ts`).

This PR fixes a separate remaining layer later in the pipeline: gateway non-streaming recombination of final reply chunks (and `/btw` side-result chunks) in `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`.

So the two PRs are adjacent and complementary, not overlapping duplicates.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Non-streaming final replies and `/btw` side results now preserve indentation when formatted content spans multiple payloads. When one chunk already ends with a single newline and the next chunk does not start with one, the gateway now preserves that authored single newline instead of forcing a blank line. The combiner also trims `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` cleanly at outer boundaries so Windows-style line endings do not leak stray carriage returns.

This fixes the flattened-output symptom reported against Matrix, but the bug itself is generic to gateway non-streaming recombination of indentation-sensitive content rather than Matrix-specific formatting.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node.js checkout
- Model/provider: n/a (gateway-side reply recombination)
- Integration/channel (if any): reproduced against the gateway non-streaming `chat.send` path; user report was on Matrix
- Relevant config (redacted): n/a

### Steps

1. Produce a non-streaming assistant reply whose formatted block is emitted across multiple final payloads.
2. Include indentation-sensitive content such as nested YAML, a nested list, or a fenced code block.
3. Observe the stored/broadcast final message text.

### Expected

- Indentation from each payload chunk is preserved in the final combined reply.

### Actual

- Leading whitespace at payload boundaries was trimmed away, flattening structured content.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Added focused regression coverage for:
- the authored single-newline separator case
- an indentation-sensitive `/btw` YAML side result
- `CRLF` boundary trimming

The linked user issue includes before/after screenshots of the real-world symptom.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: targeted gateway regression test for the single-newline recombination edge case; targeted `/btw` side-result regression test for an indented YAML block; targeted `CRLF` boundary regression coverage; targeted lint on the changed files; repo pre-commit checks during commit.
- Edge cases checked: whitespace-only payload chunks are still ignored; separator insertion still only happens when adjacent chunks are not already separated by line breaks; authored single-newline separators are preserved instead of expanded to blank lines; `CRLF` boundaries do not leave stray carriage returns.
- What you did **not** verify: live Matrix delivery against a running instance, broader streaming reply behavior outside the existing `/btw` block-side-result coverage, or full-repo test suites.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert commits `6b2461a11`, `c753cd0af`, and `d667f18f1` (or revert the PR branch changes as a unit)
- Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts`, and `src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: unexpected extra blank lines between merged payload chunks, unexpected loss of an authored single newline between chunks, stray carriage returns from `CRLF` boundaries, or lost indentation in `/btw` side results

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: preserving authored separators changes the old trim/join behavior for payloads that already end in a single newline.
  - Mitigation: this is intentional, explicitly regression-tested, and still bounded by the same whitespace-only-chunk skipping plus separator insertion only when neither side already provides a line break.
- Risk: preserving chunk text could expose `CRLF` edge cases at outer boundaries.
  - Mitigation: the combiner now trims `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` consistently at the start/end, with targeted regression coverage.
- Risk: reviewers may mistake this PR for a duplicate of #55960.
  - Mitigation: this description now names the exact layer split: `#55960` fixes directive parsing, this PR fixes gateway non-streaming recombination.

target.title: fix(feishu): pace outbound sends per target
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #14206.

Feishu outbound sends currently retry after the API returns rate-limit responses, but configured high-volume conversations can still burst several `im.message.create` / `im.message.reply` calls to the same Feishu target at once. That makes the first user-visible symptom a Feishu rate-limit error instead of a paced send queue.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds an opt-in `channels.feishu.sendRateLimit.minIntervalMs` setting, with the same setting available under `channels.feishu.accounts.<id>.sendRateLimit.minIntervalMs` for per-account overrides. When configured, Feishu text, card, image, and file message sends are paced by account + receive id type + receive id. Different Feishu targets still send independently, and the default interval remains `0` so existing installs keep their current behavior unless they opt in.

## User Impact

Operators can set a small per-target send interval to avoid Feishu per-chat send bursts while keeping cross-chat throughput. Existing retry handling for Feishu 230020 / 11232 / 429 responses remains in place for server-side limits that still occur.

## Real Behavior Proof

Exact head: `0ab71706c99ee57338dcff1be74e06eb61ee2142`.

The after-fix pacing behavior is verified at the Feishu send-runtime seam with fake timers and mocked Feishu SDK calls. The proof covers both normal message sends and initial streaming-card sends, using the exact limiter key shape that production uses: account id + receive id type + receive id.

Same-target configured pacing proof:

```text
config: sendRateLimit.minIntervalMs = 50
send #1: to oc_paced, text first
send #2: to oc_paced, text second
send #3: to oc_paced, text third
at +0ms: Feishu message.create called 1 time
at +49ms: Feishu message.create still called 1 time
at +50ms: Feishu message.create called 2 times
after final interval: Feishu message.create called 3 times
observed: callTimes[1] - callTimes[0] >= 50
observed: callTimes[2] - callTimes[1] >= 50
```

Distinct-target independence proof:

```text
config: sendRateLimit.minIntervalMs = 50
send #1: to oc_paced_a
send #2: to oc_paced_b
at +0ms: both Feishu message.create calls completed
observed receive ids: oc_paced_a, oc_paced_b
observed: calls[1].at - calls[0].at == 0
```

Streaming-card initial send proof:

```text
config: sendRateLimit.minIntervalMs = 50
reply path: two initial streaming-card reply starts for the same target
  at +0ms: Feishu message.reply called 1 time
  at +49ms: Feishu message.reply still called 1 time
  at +50ms: Feishu message.reply called 2 times
  observed: sendTimes[1] - sendTimes[0] >= 50
create path: two initial streaming-card create starts for the same target
  at +0ms: Feishu message.create called 1 time
  at +49ms: Feishu message.create still called 1 time
  at +50ms: Feishu message.create called 2 times
  observed: sendTimes[1] - sendTimes[0] >= 50
```

GitHub exact-head proof/check evidence:

- `Real behavior proof` passed on this PR head: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28164570020/job/83413199211
- Full CI passed on this PR head: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28164195535
- The relevant exact-head CI checks include `check-test-types`, `check-prod-types`, `check-lint`, `check-docs`, `check-guards`, `checks-node-compact-*`, `QA Smoke CI`, and `ci-timings-summary`, all passing.

Live Feishu API proof was not run in this local environment because no Feishu app credentials are available here. The included proof exercises the runtime scheduling and SDK request seam without sending network traffic.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/feishu/src/send.concurrent.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/send-target.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.retry.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/feishu/src/outbound.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.test.ts`
- `oxfmt --check extensions/feishu/src/send-rate-limit.ts extensions/feishu/src/send-target.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.ts extensions/feishu/src/media.ts extensions/feishu/src/config-schema.ts extensions/feishu/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.concurrent.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.ts extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.test.ts extensions/feishu/openclaw.plugin.json`
- `git diff --check`

Focused regression coverage includes:

- same-target text/card sends are serialized by the configured interval;
- distinct Feishu targets remain independent and are not serialized by each other's queue;
- media sends use the same per-target limiter;
- initial streaming-card reply and create sends use the limiter;
- invalid negative `sendRateLimit.minIntervalMs` values are rejected by schema;
- default config remains unpaced because the interval resolves to `0`.

target.title: Replace SHA-1 with SHA-256 for config fingerprinting
target.body:
## Summary

- Replace `crypto.createHash("sha1")` with `crypto.createHash("sha256")` in two files:
  - `src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts` - MCP server catalog fingerprinting
  - `src/media-understanding/apply.test.ts` - temp media file content hashing in tests

## Motivation

SHA-1 is used here for MCP server catalog fingerprinting and test file caching. The rest of the codebase consistently uses SHA-256 (e.g., `safeEqualSecret` in `src/security/secret-equal.ts`). This change aligns these two remaining SHA-1 call sites with the project's standard hash algorithm.

No functional impact - these hashes are used for cache invalidation and content-addressing, not integrity verification. SHA-256 is a drop-in replacement.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: The remaining bundle MCP fingerprint and media temp-file hash call sites no longer use SHA-1.
- **Real environment tested**: PolyU Linux workspace, OpenClaw checkout refreshed from current `origin/main`, branch `codex-refresh-59695`, inspected with the real `git` and `rg` CLIs against the patched repository.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
git diff -- src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts src/media-understanding/apply.test.ts
rg -n 'createHash\("sha1"\)' src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts src/media-understanding/apply.test.ts || true
rg -n 'createHash\("sha256"\)' src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts src/media-understanding/apply.test.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
The SHA-1 search returned no output.
src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts:68:  return crypto.createHash("sha256").update(JSON.stringify(normalizedServers)).digest("hex");
src/media-understanding/apply.test.ts:233:  const digest = crypto.createHash("sha256").update(content).digest("hex");
```

- **Observed result after fix**: Both patched files now resolve to SHA-256 call sites, and the previous `createHash("sha1")` pattern is absent from those files.
- **What was not tested**: Full project test suite was not run locally because this checkout has no installed `node_modules`; CI should run the complete project checks.

target.title: Add prototype pollution guard to resolveConfigPath
target.body:
## Summary

`resolveConfigPath` in `src/shared/config-eval.ts` traverses config objects by dot-separated path strings (e.g. `"foo.bar.baz"`), but does not check whether any path segment is a dangerous prototype key (`__proto__`, `prototype`, or `constructor`). This means a crafted path string could be used to access or leak prototype chain properties.

Other path-traversal functions in the codebase already guard against this:
- `parseConfigPath` in `src/config/config-paths.ts`
- `merge-patch.ts`, `runtime-overrides.ts`, `includes.ts`, and others

All of these use the shared `isBlockedObjectKey` utility from `src/infra/prototype-keys.ts`.

## Changes

- Import `isBlockedObjectKey` from `../infra/prototype-keys.js`
- Add a guard inside the `resolveConfigPath` loop that returns `undefined` when a blocked key is encountered
- Matches the existing security pattern used across the codebase

## Test plan

- [ ] Existing tests for `resolveConfigPath` and `isConfigPathTruthyWithDefaults` continue to pass
- [ ] Paths containing `__proto__`, `prototype`, or `constructor` segments now return `undefined`
- [ ] Normal dot-separated paths still resolve correctly

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `resolveConfigPath` now blocks prototype-key path segments before object property access while preserving normal dot-path resolution.
- **Real environment tested**: PolyU Linux workspace, OpenClaw checkout refreshed from current `origin/main`, branch `codex-refresh-59694`, inspected with the real `git` and `rg` CLIs against the patched repository.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
git diff -- src/shared/config-eval.ts src/shared/config-eval.test.ts
rg -n "isBlockedObjectKey|blocks prototype keys" src/shared/config-eval.ts src/shared/config-eval.test.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
src/shared/config-eval.test.ts:62:  it("blocks prototype keys while resolving config paths", () => {
src/shared/config-eval.ts:4:import { isBlockedObjectKey } from "../infra/prototype-keys.js";
src/shared/config-eval.ts:31:    if (isBlockedObjectKey(part)) {
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The patched resolver has a real guard before object access and the regression coverage keeps `safe.enabled` resolving while `__proto__`, `constructor.name`, and `prototype.polluted` return `undefined`.
- **What was not tested**: Full project test suite was not run locally because this checkout has no installed `node_modules`; CI should run the complete project checks.

target.title: feat: include provider/model/profile/trigger context in overloaded and rate limit error messages
target.body:
## Problem

Transient provider failures were still too terse to act on. When an overloaded or rate-limit response reached the embedded agent formatter, the visible message could omit the provider, model, active profile presence, and trigger that operators need to diagnose or retry the right path.

## Solution

This updates overloaded and rate-limit formatting to append sanitized transient context when available:

- provider/model, for example `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`
- `profile=active` when an auth profile was selected
- `trigger=...`

Provider/model and trigger remain readable. Raw profile ids, session keys, and provider request ids are not included in channel-visible copy.

The context is threaded through the embedded runner, failover, and payload builders so the same enriched copy is used for chat payloads and lifecycle/error metadata. The tests cover the context builder directly, exact overloaded/rate-limit strings, payload rendering, raw-identifier non-leakage, and the lifecycle console message without weakening the previous exact assertion.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Overloaded and rate-limit provider errors now include provider/model/profile-presence/trigger context instead of only generic retry text, while profile/session/request identifiers are omitted from visible reply text.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at PR head `9f4f5b2e2b8ae9e900b620c1013ae37fae920d88` on Node 22, executing the production embedded-agent formatter from source.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `node --import tsx --eval 'import { formatUserFacingAssistantErrorText } from "./src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers/errors.ts"; ...'` with representative overloaded and rate-limit assistant error payloads.
- **Evidence after fix:** Console output from the local OpenClaw command:

```text
overloaded: The AI service is temporarily overloaded. Please try again in a moment. [anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6, profile=active, trigger=heartbeat]
rate-limit: ⚠️ API rate limit reached. Please try again later. [openai/gpt-5.4, profile=active, trigger=cron]
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Both transient errors include safe diagnostic context in the final user-facing message without exposing raw or stable-hashed profile, session, or provider request identifiers.
- **What was not tested:** Live provider API calls; the proof uses production OpenClaw formatting code with representative provider error payloads.

## Validation

- `pnpm test -- src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.formatassistanterrortext.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/payloads.errors.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.lifecycle.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 pnpm check:changed --base upstream/main`

## Related issues

Closes #43932
Closes #51336
Closes #30262

target.title: fix(telegram): bound getChat Bot API response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`fetchTelegramChatId()` (`getChat`) on current `main` still parsed successful Telegram Bot API responses with unbounded `res.json()`. On Node, that buffers the full body before parsing, so a misbehaving or hostile Bot API endpoint can stream an oversized success payload during chat-id lookup and push the gateway into memory pressure or OOM.

That matters because this helper is used on operator-facing paths such as onboarding, routing, and chat-id normalization, and it can point at an operator-selected Bot API base URL.

This patch closes the remaining unbounded success read on the shared `getChat` lookup path while preserving the existing fail-closed contract: malformed or oversized responses still resolve to `null`.

## Why This Change Was Made

The success-path `res.json()` in `extensions/telegram/src/api-fetch.ts` now reads through `readResponseWithLimit` with a 4 MiB cap, matching the Telegram Bot API bound-read contract already used elsewhere in this hardening work. The regression test was upgraded to use real `Response` objects plus an oversized `ReadableStream`, and the loopback proof script keeps a reproducible `node:http` bytes-on-wire check for the exact production helper.

## User Impact

Normal `getChat` responses are unchanged. When a Bot API endpoint returns an abnormally large success JSON body, lookup now fails closed with `null` after cancelling near the cap instead of buffering the full response into memory.

## Evidence

Commands run:

```text
git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD -- extensions/telegram/src/api-fetch.ts extensions/telegram/src/api-fetch.test.ts scripts/proof-telegram-getchat-bound.mjs
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/api-fetch.test.ts
node --import tsx/esm scripts/proof-telegram-getchat-bound.mjs
```

Observed output:

```text
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-telegram.config.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  8 passed (8)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 7.78s

[proof] fetchTelegramChatId (getChat) production path
  cap=4194304 bytes (4 MiB), would-stream≈25165824 bytes (24 MiB)

  ok: oversized getChat fails closed with null (production fetchTelegramChatId)
  ok: server wrote 5308443 bytes, stopped before full 24 MiB stream
  ok: negative control: unbounded .json() wrote 25165853 bytes (>> 4194304)
  ok: small getChat parsed through production path (id=123456789)

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

Behavior addressed: a successful `getChat` response with no `Content-Length` must not be buffered whole once it exceeds the 4 MiB Bot API cap.

Real environment tested: local checkout on `fix/telegram-bound-getchat-response-reads`, Node `v22.22.0`, `node:http` loopback server on `127.0.0.1`, production `fetchTelegramChatId()` path.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
1. Run the targeted Telegram Vitest file.
2. Stream ~24 MiB of JSON without Content-Length from a fake Bot API endpoint.
3. Call production `fetchTelegramChatId()` and confirm it returns null before the full body drains.
4. Run the negative control with unbounded `response.json()` against the same stream.
5. Re-run `fetchTelegramChatId()` against a small valid response.
```

Evidence after fix: the bounded path stopped after `5308443` bytes and returned `null`, while the negative control drained `25165853` bytes from the same oversized stream.

Observed result after fix: oversized success bodies now fail closed near the cap, and normal small `getChat` envelopes still parse to the expected chat id.

What was not tested: a live Telegram cloud API call and a broader Telegram extension suite beyond `extensions/telegram/src/api-fetch.test.ts`.

target.title: fix(telegram): bound Bot API response reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Three Telegram Bot API response paths use unbounded `await response.json()` on the **success** path, leaving the gateway exposed to OOM if a misbehaving or hostile endpoint streams an oversized response body:

| File | Endpoint | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| `extensions/telegram/src/probe.ts` | `getMe`, `getWebhookInfo` | Called on every probe / health-check |
| `extensions/telegram/src/audit-membership-runtime.ts` | `getChatMember` | Called during group-membership audit |
| `extensions/telegram/src/telegram-ingress-worker.runtime.ts` | `getUpdates` | **Hot path** — called every polling cycle in the ingress worker |

Legitimate Telegram Bot API responses for these endpoints are tiny (< 1 KiB for `getMe`/`getChatMember`, < 500 KiB for 100 `getUpdates`). An unbounded read allows any interceptor or rogue endpoint to force the gateway to buffer an arbitrarily large body before returning control.

This continues the response-limit campaign already merged for Discord, xAI, fal, MiniMax, OpenRouter, embeddings, speech, and other surfaces.

## Changes

* `extensions/telegram/src/probe.ts`
  - Imports `readResponseWithLimit` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/response-limit-runtime`
  - Adds `TELEGRAM_BOT_API_MAX_RESPONSE_BYTES = 4 MiB` constant
  - Replaces `await meRes.json()` (`getMe`) with `readResponseWithLimit` + `JSON.parse`
  - Replaces `await webhookRes.json()` (`getWebhookInfo`) with `readResponseWithLimit` + `JSON.parse`

* `extensions/telegram/src/audit-membership-runtime.ts`
  - Imports `readResponseWithLimit` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/response-limit-runtime`
  - Adds `TELEGRAM_BOT_API_MAX_RESPONSE_BYTES = 4 MiB` constant
  - Replaces `await res.json()` (`getChatMember`) with `readResponseWithLimit` + `JSON.parse`

* `extensions/telegram/src/telegram-ingress-worker.runtime.ts`
  - Imports `readResponseWithLimit` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/response-limit-runtime`
  - Adds `TELEGRAM_GET_UPDATES_MAX_RESPONSE_BYTES = 4 MiB` constant
  - Replaces `await response.json()` (`getUpdates`) with `readResponseWithLimit` + `JSON.parse`

* `extensions/telegram/src/probe.test.ts`
  - Adds a `vi.mock` pass-through for `openclaw/plugin-sdk/response-limit-runtime` so existing
    plain-object fetch mocks continue to work without requiring real `Response` body streams

* `scripts/proof-telegram-bound.mjs` — local proof script (see Evidence below)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Unbounded `response.json()` on Telegram Bot API success paths — a misbehaving endpoint can stream an arbitrarily large body, exhausting gateway heap before the read returns
- **Real environment tested:** `node:http` `createServer` on `127.0.0.1:0`, Node v22+, macOS
- **Exact steps run after this patch:**
  ```
  node --import tsx/esm scripts/proof-telegram-bound.mjs
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:** see Evidence section below
- **Observed result:** `readResponseWithLimit` cancelled the stream after reading ~1.1 MiB (server wrote ~1.4 MiB total including HTTP framing); the unbounded `.json()` negative-control read the full 25 MiB; a normal 99-byte happy-path response parsed correctly
- **What was not tested:** live Telegram Bot API calls; the `api-fetch.ts` `getChat` path (already guarded by `.catch(() => null)`, lower risk)
- **Proof limitations:** Byte tracking is server-side write counters. TCP framing adds overhead so server-written bytes are slightly above the 1 MiB cap; the reader itself stops at the cap.

shen@192 openclaw % node --import tsx/esm scripts/proof-telegram-bound.mjs


[proof] Telegram Bot API response-limit
  cap=1048576 bytes (1 MiB), would-stream≈25165824 bytes (24 MiB)

  ok: oversized body rejected (threw=true)
  ok: error message contains limit info: "Content too large: 1072391 bytes (limit: 1048576 bytes)"
  ok: server wrote ≈1572885 bytes, well below 24 MiB (stream was cancelled early)
  ok: negative control: unbounded .json() caused server to write ≈25165847 bytes (>> 1048576)
  ok: small response parsed correctly (ok=true id=123456789)
  ok: result bytes within cap (99 < 1048576)

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
shen@192 openclaw % pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/probe.test.ts

$ node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/telegram/src/probe.test.ts
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-telegram.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8 /Users/shen/Documents/GitHub/openclaw

 ✓  extension-telegram  extensions/telegram/src/probe.test.ts (12 tests) 19ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  12 passed (12)
   Start at  21:56:43
   Duration  2.09s (transform 1.17s, setup 253ms, import 1.74s, tests 19ms, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 5.36s


## Evidence

```
node --import tsx/esm scripts/proof-telegram-bound.mjs

[proof] Telegram Bot API response-limit
  cap=1048576 bytes (1 MiB), would-stream≈25165824 bytes (24 MiB)

  ok: oversized body rejected (threw=true)
  ok: error message contains limit info: "Content too large: 1072391 bytes (limit: 1048576 bytes)"
  ok: server wrote ≈1441813 bytes, well below 24 MiB (stream was cancelled early)
  ok: negative control: unbounded .json() caused server to write ≈25165847 bytes (>> 1048576)
  ok: small response parsed correctly (ok=true id=123456789)
  ok: result bytes within cap (99 < 1048576)

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

```
pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/probe.test.ts

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  12 passed (12)
```

## Tests and validation

- `pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/probe.test.ts` — 12/12 pass
- `pnpm test extensions/telegram` — 2214/2218 pass (4 pre-existing failures in `bot.create-telegram-bot.channel-post-media.test.ts`, confirmed on `main` before this change)
- `scripts/proof-telegram-bound.mjs` — ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
- Regression test added: N/A — size-cap behavior covered by the proof script; existing probe tests verify the happy-path JSON round-trip through the mock

## Risk checklist

- Did user-visible behavior change? **No** — only affects what happens when a Bot API endpoint returns an abnormally oversized response body; all normal responses are well under 4 MiB
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? **No**
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? **No** — defense-in-depth addition only
- Highest-risk area: `telegram-ingress-worker.runtime.ts` (`getUpdates` hot path); new import from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/response-limit-runtime` in the worker thread
- Mitigation: `readResponseWithLimit` uses only standard Web Streams + `Buffer` APIs available in Node.js worker threads; the 4 MiB cap is far above the largest realistic Bot API response

## Current review state

- Next action: awaiting maintainer review
- Bot comments addressed: none yet
- AI-assisted (Claude / Cursor Agent); human-run proof script on macOS

target.title: [codex] harden Telegram transient recovery
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw can stay locally healthy while Telegram appears unresponsive when Telegram Bot API calls fail in two different transient ways:

- Bot API HTTP 5xx responses from polling/setup paths crossed the isolated Telegram worker boundary as fatal server errors, causing ingress exits and supervisor backoff.
- Slow or hanging provider sends could outlive the outbound message gateway client default timeout. The caller would abandon the request at 10s even though Telegram send handling is bounded at 60s, leaving ambiguous delivery state and no visible proof in the source chat.

The fix classifies Telegram Bot API HTTP 5xx responses as recoverable for polling/setup/control-plane contexts while keeping non-idempotent `send` context conservative. It also aligns the outbound message gateway default timeout with bounded provider sends, so callers receive the provider success/failure instead of an early local gateway timeout.

## Summary

- classify Telegram Bot API HTTP 5xx responses as recoverable for polling/setup/control-plane contexts
- keep `send` context conservative to avoid duplicate visible messages
- increase the outbound message gateway default timeout from 10s to 75s
- document the `Bad Gateway` troubleshooting behavior for Telegram polling/startup instability

## Evidence

- Focused regression: `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/telegram/src/network-errors.test.ts` passes: 53 tests
- Focused timeout contract: `pnpm exec vitest run src/infra/outbound/message-gateway-options.test.ts` passes: 3 tests
- Local observed failure pattern: gateway health remained live, but Telegram logs repeated `Bad Gateway | Telegram ingress worker exited with code 1` after Bot API timeouts/502s
- Local proof after applying the same classifier hotpatch earlier in the incident: safe gateway restart, Telegram account connected, and `openclaw message send --channel telegram ... --json` returned `ok: true` with a Telegram message id
- Follow-up local proof for the timeout alignment: the installed hotpatch changed the visible failure from `GatewayTransportError: gateway timeout after 10000ms` to the provider-level `OutboundDeliveryError: Network request for sendMessage failed!` after the bounded provider timeout
- Sanitized token-specific probe evidence: dummy/fake-token Telegram `getMe` probes returned immediately, while the configured real bot token established TLS and then timed out with zero bytes. This points to a Telegram-side/token-specific provider hang; the PR keeps OpenClaw behavior bounded and diagnosable rather than wedged or ambiguous.
- Local `pnpm check:changed` from the earlier PR pass did not execute checks because the delegated Blacksmith Testbox `crabbox` binary failed its own sanity check before running

target.title: fix(gateway): tools.effective fails for global sessions on non-default agents
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where operators viewing the Control UI tools panel for a **global** session on a **non-default agent** would get `agent id "<requested>" does not match session agent "<default>"` and see no effective tool list.

Repro: call `tools.effective` with `{ sessionKey: "global", agentId: "work" }` while `work` is a configured agent and the default agent is `main`.

## Why This Change Was Made

Two wiring gaps caused the false mismatch on global sessions:

1. `resolveTrustedToolsEffectiveContext` called `loadSessionEntry(sessionKey)` without forwarding the validated `requestedAgentId`, so `global` loaded the default agent store.
2. `resolveSessionAgentId` was called without `agentId`, so a loaded `global` row still resolved to the default agent id before the match guard ran.

The fix mirrors sibling gateway RPCs (`sessions.get`, `chat.history`): pass `{ agentId: requestedAgentId }` into `loadSessionEntry`, and pass the same `agentId` into `resolveSessionAgentId`. The post-load match guard is unchanged.

## User Impact

Operators can open the effective tools inventory for global sessions scoped to any configured agent (not only the default agent). No config or protocol changes.

## Evidence

### Focused unit + integration tests (real session store, unmocked `loadSessionEntry`)

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  src/gateway/server-methods/tools-effective.test.ts \
  src/gateway/server-methods/tools-effective.global-agent.integration.test.ts

# Test Files  4 passed (4)
# Tests       44 passed (44)
```

Integration proof (`tools-effective.global-agent.integration.test.ts`):
- Seeds separate `main` and `work` global session stores on disk (`session.scope=global`, `{agentId}` store template).
- Calls the real `tools.effective` handler with `{ sessionKey: "global", agentId: "work" }`.
- Asserts success and that inventory resolves with `agentId: "work"` and `modelId: "work-model"` from the **work** agent store (not the default agent store).

### Before/after behavior (integration repro)

| Step | Before fix | After fix |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `loadSessionEntry("global")` without agent id | Loads default agent `global` row | — |
| `resolveSessionAgentId({ sessionKey: "global" })` | Returns `main` | — |
| Request `{ sessionKey: "global", agentId: "work" }` | `agent id "work" does not match session agent "main"` | `ok: true`, inventory scoped to `work` |

Unit regression (`tools-effective.test.ts`): asserts `loadSessionEntry("global", { agentId: "work" })` and `resolveSessionAgentId` receives `agentId: "work"`.

target.title: fix(launchd): verify profile updater jobs by metadata
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

A macOS update handoff can leave behind a profile-scoped launchd label like `ai.openclaw.<profile>.update.<timestamp>`, which can keep rerunning `openclaw update --yes` after the gateway is already healthy.

PR #96924 was closed because a label-only matcher could also classify a legitimate gateway profile whose name contains `.update.<timestamp>`. This replacement keeps the bug fix but removes that false-positive path.

## Why This Change Was Made

Profile-scoped update labels are now treated as candidates only. Before stale-job reporting or disabling, OpenClaw confirms the launchd job through metadata:

- explicit `OPENCLAW_UPDATE_RUN_HANDOFF=1` in the LaunchAgent environment, or
- LaunchAgent `ProgramArguments` that look like an OpenClaw update command with `update --yes`, and not a gateway command.

Legacy `ai.openclaw.update.*` and `ai.openclaw.manual-update.<digits>` behavior remains unchanged. The scanner process environment marker is intentionally not trusted for other profile-scoped labels.

## User Impact

OpenClaw can detect and disable the stuck profile-scoped updater loop, while `status`/`doctor` will not recommend disabling gateway jobs whose profile names happen to look update-shaped.

## Evidence

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/daemon/launchd.ts src/daemon/launchd.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/daemon/launchd.test.ts` -> 94 passed
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/daemon/launchd.ts src/daemon/launchd.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `git diff --check`

### macOS launchd behavior proof

Ran from this branch on macOS with two temporary LaunchAgents under `gui/501`, both created with `RunAtLoad=false` and removed immediately after capture. The positive control used profile-scoped updater metadata; the negative control used a gateway command under a label that still matches `ai.openclaw.<profile>.update.<timestamp>`.

```text
macOS launchd profile-updater proof
domain=gui/501
temporary labels:
  updater=ai.openclaw.proofcody.update.20260627T160051Z
  gateway_control=ai.openclaw.dev.team.update.20260627T160051Z
launchctl list matching labels:
  -	0	ai.openclaw.dev.team.update.20260627T160051Z
  -	0	ai.openclaw.proofcody.update.20260627T160051Z
metadata excerpts:
  updater ProgramArguments=[/opt/homebrew/bin/openclaw, update, --yes, --json]
```

target.title: fix(bedrock): apply model maxTokens to adaptive-thinking and Fable 5 requests (fixes #97176)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #97176.

Amazon Bedrock Claude models using adaptive thinking (Opus 4.8, Fable 5) are hard-capped at 4096 output tokens. At higher thinking levels the thinking block alone consumes the budget, causing `stop_reason: "length"` before any answer or tool call is delivered.

## Root Cause

`resolveSimpleBedrockOptions` has three Claude branches. The **non-adaptive Claude branch** already calls `adjustMaxTokensForThinking()` which factors in `model.maxTokens`. But the **Fable 5 branch** and **adaptive-thinking branch** both returned `base` with reasoning/thinkingBudgets set but never derived `maxTokens` from `model.maxTokens` — leaving it at the 4096 default.

## Changes Made

- `extensions/amazon-bedrock/stream.runtime.ts`: Added `resolveModelBackedMaxTokens()` helper (`baseMaxTokens ?? model.maxTokens`). Applied in Fable 5 and adaptive-thinking branches. Moved `streamSimpleBedrock` after `resolveSimpleBedrockOptions` for clarity.
- `extensions/amazon-bedrock/stream.runtime.test.ts`: Added 10 regression tests covering all branches, effort levels, edge cases, and integration-level API request verification.

## Evidence

- **Unit tests:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/amazon-bedrock/stream.runtime.test.ts` — 30/30 passed (2 consecutive runs, no flakes)
- **Lint:** `npx oxlint --tsconfig tsconfig.json extensions/amazon-bedrock/stream.runtime.ts extensions/amazon-bedrock/stream.runtime.test.ts` — clean
- **Lockfile:** unchanged

## Test coverage

| Scenario | Option-level | Integration |
|----------|:---:|:---:|
| adaptive-thinking: model.maxTokens=200k | ✅ | ✅ |
| adaptive-thinking: explicit caller cap (8k) | ✅ | — |
| adaptive-thinking: medium/xhigh/low effort | ✅ | — |
| adaptive-thinking: model.maxTokens undefined → 4096 | ✅ | — |
| Fable 5: model.maxTokens=128k | ✅ | ✅ |
| Fable 5: default reasoning (no options) | ✅ | — |
| Fable 5: explicit caller cap (16k) | ✅ | — |
| Fable 5: model.maxTokens undefined → 4096 | ✅ | — |

## Real behavior proof

Integration tests capture the actual Bedrock Converse API request sent after the fix:

```
$ streamSimpleBedrock(opus-4-8, {reasoning: "high"})
→ inferenceConfig.maxTokens = 200000 ✅ (was 4096 before fix)

$ streamSimpleBedrock(fable-5, {reasoning: "high"})
→ inferenceConfig.maxTokens = 128000 ✅ (was 4096 before fix)

$ streamSimpleBedrock(fable-5, {maxTokens: 16000})
→ inferenceConfig.maxTokens = 16000 ✅ (caller cap preserved)
```

**What was not tested:** Live Bedrock API calls (fix is in the shared option-resolver layer, unit-level verification suffices). Fable 5 without the Bedrock contract path (direct `streamBedrock` callers — `streamSimpleBedrock` is the canonical entry point).

## AI Assistance 🤖

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Co-Authored-By**: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
- **Human confirmed understanding**: Yes

target.title: improve(control-ui): expose linear session positions in chat picker
target.body:
Related: #82450

## What Problem This Solves

Resolves a first slice of the accessibility problem where blind and keyboard users navigating the existing Control UI chat session picker did not get a linear row position or a clear current-session cue from each session option.

This is intentionally related to #82450 rather than closing it: the larger Linear Persistent Workspace Mode still needs product decisions beyond this picker affordance.

## Why This Change Was Made

The chat session picker now gives each loaded option a stable visible ordinal marker and an explicit accessible label that includes current-session state plus its position in the loaded list. This keeps the existing `sessions.list` search, pagination, filtering, and storage behavior unchanged.

ClawSweeper follow-up: the new current-session and loaded-list-position phrases are routed through `chat.selectors.*` i18n keys, and all generated non-English locale outputs now carry translations for those keys with `fallbacks=0` metadata.

## User Impact

Screen-reader users can hear labels such as `Alpha planning, current session, session 1 of 2 in the loaded list` while navigating the picker, and sighted keyboard users get matching linear row numbers in the existing picker UI.

target.title: fix(workspace): prevent false Missing labels on existing files and fix panel layout overlap
target.body: Fixes two bugs in the session workspace side panel where files referenced by tool calls outside the workspace root were incorrectly shown as "Missing",and the panel layout would visually overlap when many files were present. Note: the root-path leading-dot issue reported in the same issue (#96660) was already resolved separately. The remaining two bugs were reproduced with screenshots attached below. Fixes #96660 ## What Problem This Solves - False "Missing" badge on existing files Fixes an issue where the "Expand Session Workspace" side panel would show a red "Missing" badge on files that actually exist on disk (e.g. /tmp/foo.txt). Any tool-call path outside the workspace root was unconditionally flagged as missing because resolveTouchedFilePath returns undefined for external paths, and statWorkspacePath can fail to reach them through the workspace sandbox. - Side panel layout overlap Fixes a layout bug where the section headers, search input, and file rows in the side panel would visually overlap when the file list grew large, making the panel unreadable. - Regression in sessions.files.get error type The directStat fallback (item 1) introduced a regression: when sessions.files.get requested content for a file outside the workspace, directStat returned { missing: false } without content, causing the handler to emit session_file_too_large instead of the correct session_file_not_found. Fixed by skipping directStat when includeContent is requested, since we can never safely serve file content for paths outside the workspace sandbox. ## Why This Change Was Made A directStat helper was added that uses fs.existsSync + fs.statSync as a best-effort fallback before marking a file as missing. This runs in two places: (1) when the resolved path is outside the workspace root, and (2) when the workspace sandbox fails to stat the path. The fallback is only used for the list view (sessions.files.list), where content is not needed — stat metadata alone is sufficient. When content is requested (sessions.files.get), paths that can't be reached through the sandbox are treated as not found, preserving the original error semantics. The CSS fix adds flex-shrink: 0 to .chat-workspace-rail__section and its title so flex containers cannot compress sections below their content height, which was causing the overlapping layers. ## User Impact Users opening the session workspace side panel will no longer see false "Missing" badges on files that exist on disk but are outside the workspace root. The panel layout remains stable and readable even with many files, artifacts, and browser entries. ## Evidence Verified locally by creating files at `/tmp/a.txt` ... via tool calls, then confirming the workspace panel shows them without the red "Missing" badge in both Changed and Read sections. The panel layout no longer overlaps after refreshing with many files present. the screenshots attached below.

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes the low-risk documentation/index portion of #59728 by adding a discoverable `scripts/README.md` for the current `scripts/` directory.

The issue also proposes a wholesale scripts directory reorganization. This PR intentionally does not move or rename scripts because those paths are referenced by `package.json`, GitHub Actions, docs, and maintainer runbooks. ## Why This Change Was Made

target.title: fix: honor Bedrock adaptive max token caps
target.body:
Closes #97176

AI-assisted with Codex.

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users running Bedrock Claude Fable or adaptive-thinking Claude models could have Converse requests capped at the core default 4096 output tokens when the resolved model metadata had a larger `maxTokens` cap. At higher thinking effort, the thinking block could consume that whole budget and end the turn at `length` before a visible answer or tool call.

## Why This Change Was Made

Bedrock simple-option resolution now carries the model-backed `maxTokens` into Fable, mandatory adaptive, and explicit adaptive-thinking requests when the caller did not set `maxTokens`. Explicit caller `maxTokens` remains the override path, and the existing adaptive thinking payload and temperature behavior are preserved.

## User Impact

Bedrock adaptive-thinking Claude requests can use the configured model/catalog output-token cap instead of silently falling back to 4096, reducing length-truncated turns for long or high-thinking generations.

## Evidence

- Red test first: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/amazon-bedrock/stream.runtime.test.ts` failed before the fix with 4 assertions showing `inferenceConfig: {}` instead of model-backed `maxTokens`.
- `env OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_CHANGED_LANES_RAW_SYNC=1 CI=1 PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false pnpm check:changed -- extensions/amazon-bedrock/stream.runtime.ts extensions/amazon-bedrock/stream.runtime.test.ts`

target.title: fix(exec): remove misleading policy claim from approval prompt (#97069)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes P2 issue #97069: When an exec command includes shell redirection (e.g., `openclaw --version 2>&1`), Allow Always is unavailable because the command is **non-persistable** (one-shot). However, the approval prompt incorrectly says:

> "The effective approval policy requires approval every time, so Allow Always is unavailable."

This is misleading even when the effective gateway exec policy is `security=allowlist` and `ask=on-miss`.

## Why This Change Was Made

`buildApprovalPendingMessage` always assumed `allow-always` exclusion meant `ask === "always"`. But `allow-always` can also be excluded for non-persistable commands. Replaced the misleading message with a neutral one.

## User Impact

Users running redirected commands see an accurate message. Plain commands continue to offer Allow Always normally.

## Evidence

### Real behavior proof (`npx tsx scripts/proof-issue-97069.ts`)

```
TEST 2: Redirected command — Allow Always NOT available

Command:     openclaw --version 2>&1
Decisions:   allow-once, deny (no allow-always)

BEFORE FIX:
  "The effective approval policy requires approval every time,
   so Allow Always is unavailable."
  → Misleading: user thinks policy is ask=always

AFTER FIX:
  "Allow Always is unavailable for this command."
  → Accurate: doesn't blame the policy

Contains old message: NO (correct) ✓
Contains new message: YES (correct) ✓
```

- 1 line changed in `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.ts`

target.title: src/agents/runtime/proxy.ts: bound non-2xx response.json reads and add SSE pending buffer cap plus idle timeout re-arm
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Fix classification: root-cause reliability fix for unbounded proxy response reads.
- Root cause: the proxy response boundary trusted upstream bodies to stay small and make progress; non-2xx responses used `response.json()` without a byte limit, and the SSE parser waited indefinitely on `reader.read()` while previously maintaining proxy-local byte accounting instead of reusing the canonical SSE byte guard.
- Why this is root-cause fix: bytes are now bounded at the stream boundary before parser/message-delivery state changes: non-2xx bodies go through `readResponseWithLimit`, SSE success bytes go through the shared `createSseByteGuard`, and each SSE read has a per-read idle timeout that is re-armed after progress.
- Patch quality notes: the follow-up replaces duplicate proxy-local total-byte accounting with `createSseByteGuard`, fixes the CI oxlint findings, and keeps test-only reader casts isolated to deterministic pending/cancel stream fixtures.

Why does this matter now?

- Why it matters / User impact: a broken or malicious proxy can no longer make OpenClaw buffer an oversized error body, keep a proxied message stream hanging forever without bytes, or deliver unlimited SSE bytes before the client notices.

What is the intended outcome?

- Maintainer-ready confidence: high for this scoped PR; the current head has focused proxy tests, CI-equivalent oxlint, focused TypeScript validation, and a real local HTTP proxy-style proof covering normal, overflow, and idle paths.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- What did NOT change: no proxy request payload shape, SSE event schema, public API, user config, default value, migration, provider routing, auth behavior, or server-side proxy protocol changed.
- Out of scope: adding user-configurable caps/timeouts, changing gateway/proxy server behavior, changing provider-specific streaming code, changing event wire format, or adding protocol negotiation.

What does success look like?

Normal proxy responses continue to parse the same events, while oversized or stalled proxy bodies fail with bounded errors and release/cancel the response reader instead of growing memory or waiting indefinitely.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Architecture / source-of-truth check: proxy response bytes now use the repository's canonical `createSseByteGuard` for SSE byte accounting/cancellation semantics, while `streamProxy` owns only the proxy-specific idle timeout, pending-line cap, and error messages.
- Related open PR / same-contract scan: ClawSweeper noted overlap with #96768 and #97191; this PR is scoped to `streamProxy` read bounds and does not change the shared protocol, schema, or provider routing contract. Maintainers may sequence overlapping stream-guard PRs, but this branch now uses the canonical byte guard instead of introducing a competing accounting implementation.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Problem-mode PR; no GitHub issue number.

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related context: reported hardening gap in `src/agents/runtime/proxy.ts` covering bounded non-2xx JSON reads, SSE pending/total byte caps, and SSE read idle timeout re-arm.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No maintainer request linked in the local task context.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `streamProxy` now returns bounded results for oversized non-2xx JSON error bodies, oversized SSE success bodies, and idle SSE reads instead of buffering or awaiting unbounded proxy responses.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw PR worktree using a real `http.createServer` proxy-style `/api/stream` endpoint and the production `streamProxy` implementation; endpoint and token are redacted in output.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `corepack pnpm exec tsx <evidence>/proxy-real-http-proof.ts` via `daily_fix.sh run-validation --name proxy-real-http-proof`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
{"scenario":"normal-stream","endpoint":"<local-http-proxy>","stopReason":"stop","errorMessage":"","durationMs":83}
{"scenario":"sse-overflow","endpoint":"<local-http-proxy>","stopReason":"error","errorMessage":"Proxy SSE stream exceeded 16777216 bytes","durationMs":9291}
{"scenario":"error-body-overflow","endpoint":"<local-http-proxy>","stopReason":"error","errorMessage":"Proxy error body exceeded 16777216 bytes","durationMs":179}
{"scenario":"idle-timeout","endpoint":"<local-http-proxy>","stopReason":"error","errorMessage":"Proxy SSE stream stalled: no data received for 120000ms","durationMs":120009}
```

- Observed result after fix: the real proxy-style run confirms one normal terminal stream and three bounded failure paths: total SSE overflow, non-2xx error-body overflow, and read idle timeout.
- What was not tested: no external hosted proxy service was used; the proof uses a local HTTP server so the response body is real HTTP but deterministic, redacted, and does not require provider credentials.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: a full repository `tsc --noEmit --pretty false` previously hit local heap OOM, so current validation uses focused TypeScript over the touched files plus CI-equivalent oxlint and focused runtime tests.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this patch, the affected paths used unbounded `response.json()` and unbounded/idle `reader.read()` behavior; the PR regression tests lock the corrected failure behavior.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- Target test file: `src/agents/runtime/proxy.test.ts`.

Validation commands:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/runtime/proxy.test.ts
node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --threads=8
node --max-old-space-size=4096 ./node_modules/typescript/bin/tsc -p .tsconfig.proxy-check.tmp.json --noEmit --pretty false
corepack pnpm exec tsx <evidence>/proxy-real-http-proof.ts
```

Validation results on current head:

```text
proxy-pr-vitest-postcommit: pass, 9 tests passed
proxy-pr-oxlint-ci-postcommit: pass, CI-equivalent oxlint shards completed
proxy-pr-focused-tsc-postcommit: pass
proxy-real-http-proof: pass, normal/overflow/idle proxy-style scenarios observed
```

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Scenario locked in: non-2xx error bodies over 16 MiB cancel with `Proxy error body exceeded 16777216 bytes`; SSE success bytes over 16 MiB cancel before parser dispatch; an idle SSE read returns `Proxy SSE stream stalled: no data received for 120000ms`; a later chunk before the idle window expires proves the timeout is re-armed instead of applied once globally.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the regression tests and production change target only the proxy response boundary, reuse the canonical SSE byte guard for total-byte accounting, and avoid new config, schema, protocol, provider routing, or downstream message-delivery changes.

If no test was added, why not?

Regression tests were added in `src/agents/runtime/proxy.test.ts` and real HTTP proxy-style proof was recorded for the reviewer-requested behavior path.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes, but only for malformed, oversized, or stalled proxy responses: they now fail earlier with explicit bounded errors instead of buffering or waiting indefinitely.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No auth/secrets/tool execution behavior changed; the network response read loop now has local byte and idle-time guardrails.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Risk labels considered: message-delivery, runtime streaming, compatibility.
- Risk explanation: the touched code sits on the proxy message-delivery path and introduces fail-closed behavior for proxy responses that exceed 16 MiB or produce no bytes for 120 seconds. The compatible path for successful SSE events is unchanged, and total-byte cancellation now reuses the existing canonical SSE guard to avoid drift from sibling parsers.

How is that risk mitigated?

- Why acceptable: normal small responses still follow the existing parser path, oversized/stalled responses now fail before further parser dispatch, the byte cap uses shared guard semantics, and validation covers both malformed/stalled responses and an active normal stream that should complete successfully.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

This update addresses the visible author-side blockers, then should be submitted through `submit-pr` and allowed to settle.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

Author-side updates addressed:

- RF-001 / RF-002 / RF-005 / RF-007: real local HTTP proxy-style proof added for normal stream, SSE overflow, error-body overflow, and idle timeout.
- RF-003: compatibility/message-delivery risk is described in the risk checklist.
- RF-004 / RF-008: total SSE byte accounting now reuses `createSseByteGuard`.
- RF-006: overlap with #96768 and #97191 is disclosed for maintainer sequencing.
- CI `check-lint`: reproduced as current-PR oxlint failures and fixed; CI-equivalent oxlint shards now pass locally.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

ClawSweeper's proof and canonical SSE byte guard findings from the initial bot comment are addressed in this update.

## Summary
- separate local gateway reachability from follow-up detail/read probe failures
- add a bounded direct status-RPC fallback or degraded classification for local status/probe paths when the default probe times out or returns unknown capability but the gateway is otherwise RPC-capable

## Scope
This intentionally excludes unauthenticated loopback auth relaxation from #64400, which is routed to central security triage. The fix should stay in the ordinary status/probe classification and fallback path.
